content:
First note that “\(n^2\) has the same remainder modulo 7, 11 and 13” means

\[
n^2\equiv r\pmod7,\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{11},\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{13}
\]

for some \(r\).  Since \(r\pmod7\) already lies in \(\{0,1,\dots,6\}\), we only need
\[
n^2\equiv r\pmod{1001},\quad 0\le r\le6,
\]
where \(1001=7\cdot11\cdot13\).

1.  Count the solutions mod 1001.  Writing
\[
S \;=\;\#\{\,a\pmod{1001}:\;a^2\equiv r\pmod{1001}\text{ for some }r\in\{0,\dots,6\}\},
\]
we use the Chinese remainder theorem.  For each \(r=0,\dots,6\) set
\[
A_7(r)=\#\{x\pmod7:x^2\equiv r\pmod7\},\;
A_{11}(r)=\#\{x\pmod{11}:x^2\equiv r\},\;
A_{13}(r)=\#\{x\pmod{13}:x^2\equiv r\}.
\]
Then
\[
S \;=\;\sum_{r=0}^6 A_7(r)\,A_{11}(r)\,A_{13}(r).
\]
One finds by direct tabulation of squares mod 7,11,13:
\[
A_7(0,1,2,4)=(1,2,2,2),\quad\text{zero otherwise},
\]
\[
A_{11}(0,1,3,4,5)=(1,2,2,2,2),\quad\text{zero for }r=2,6,
\]
\[
A_{13}(0,1,3,4,9,10,12)=(1,2,2,2,2,2,2),\quad\text{zero for }r=2,5,6.
\]
Hence only \(r=0,1,4\) contribute:
\[
S \;=\;A_7(0)A_{11}(0)A_{13}(0)\;+\;A_7(1)A_{11}(1)A_{13}(1)
\;+\;A_7(4)A_{11}(4)A_{13}(4)
\;=\;1\cdot1\cdot1\;+\;2\cdot2\cdot2\;+\;2\cdot2\cdot2
\;=\;1+8+8=17.
\]

2.  Now among the positive \(n<2018\) there are exactly two full residue‐mod 1001
cycles (those give \(2\cdot17=34\) solutions) plus the “tail” \(n=2003,2004,\dots,2017\),
which correspond to residues \(1,2,\dots,15\) mod 1001.  A quick check shows that
among \(n=1,2,\dots,15\) only \(n=1,2\) satisfy
\(\,n^2\bmod7=n^2\bmod11=n^2\bmod13\).  Hence the tail contributes 2 more.

In total there are
\[
2\cdot17 \;+\;2 \;=\;34+2\;=\;\boxed{36}
\]
such positive integers \(n<2018\).
